title: The Worked-Example Library
intro:
APPENDIX D · PRS PRESENTATION LIBRARY

The Worked-Example Library

Mission: Give every advisor a memorized PRS template for the eight most common repair categories. Drill these and the structure becomes instinct.

EXAMPLES
8 CATEGORIES
FORMAT
10-STEP ISO
USE
DAILY DRILL
CALIBRATION
VS WATER PUMP
How to Use Brakes Suspension Cooling Transmission Electrical Tires Alignment A/C

sections.title: How to Use This Library
sections.html:
How to Use This Library

Each example below is built on the same 10-step ISO Safety Presentation template anatomized in Module 6 (the original water-pump example). The structure never changes. What changes is the technical content — the Problem, the Risk, the Solution, the Investment, and the warranty language — tuned to each repair category.

Use these examples three ways: (1) Drill material — one example per morning standup in Phase 2 of the rollout. (2) Calibration reference — when a coach scores an advisor’s PRS, compare against the matching example here. (3) New-hire training — the advisor must deliver each one from memory before being released to live presentations.

PRO TIPThe pattern stays constant. Memorize the pattern, vary the technical content.

Every example below follows the exact same 10 beats: Warm Open · Co-Review · Media Confirm · Concern · Good News · More Good News · Risk → Solution · Investment · Coverage · Tee-Up. Once the pattern is automatic, the advisor only has to think about the repair — the structure carries itself.

sections.title: 1 · Brakes — Front Pads & Rotors (Safety)
sections.html:
1 · Brakes — Front Pads & Rotors (Safety)

Scenario: Customer came in for a brake squeal. DVI confirms front pads at 2mm with rotor scoring.

SCRIPTBase-1 ISO · Front Brakes

1. Warm Opening & Time Check. “Hi [Customer], this is [Advisor] from [Shop]. Thanks for your patience — is now a good time to go over your vehicle?”

2. Advisor Co-Review. “I personally walked the vehicle with our certified tech so I could give you a clear picture of what’s going on with the braking system.”

3. Media Confirmation. “Were you able to view the photos and the brake-measurement video on the digital inspection report I sent?”

4. Concern. “You brought the vehicle in because of the squealing when braking. We’ve fully inspected the system.”

5. Good News. “The good news is the rear brakes still have plenty of life left, and the brake fluid tested clean.”

6. More Good News (discovery). “More good news — we caught the front brakes right at the safety threshold and not past it, which means we can replace the components without damage to the caliper or hydraulic system.”

7. Risk → Solution. “If left as-is, the front pads are at 2 millimeters — that means metal-on-metal is the next stop, which destroys the rotors and can compromise stopping distance. The proper repair is to replace the front pads and rotors, perform a full brake system service including hardware and fluid bleed, and road-test under load to confirm.”

8. Total Investment. “The total investment for today’s work, parts and labor included, is $[XXX].”

9. Coverage. “This work is fully covered by our 3-year / 36,000-mile nationwide warranty on both parts and labor.”

10. Tee-Up & Callback. “While your vehicle is here, we’ll finish the full bumper-to-bumper inspection and I’ll reach out if anything else needs attention. Is this still the best number to reach you?”

PSYCHOLOGY DECODERWhy “metal-on-metal” lands harder than “worn out.”

“Worn out” is abstract. “Metal-on-metal” is a vivid mental image that the customer can hear in their head. Concrete sensory language activates the same brain regions as the actual event, which is why specific physical descriptions move customers to authorize faster than generic ones.

sections.title: 2 · Suspension — CV Axle Boot Leak (Yellow → Red Progression)
sections.html:
2 · Suspension — CV Axle Boot Leak (Yellow → Red Progression)

Scenario: Customer came in for an oil change. DVI finds CV axle boot torn with grease slung on inner suspension.

SCRIPTBase-2 Additional Concern · CV Axle

1. Warm Opening. “Hi [Customer], this is [Advisor] from [Shop]. I have an update on the inspection — is now a good time?”

2. Co-Review. “I just finished walking the vehicle with our tech so I could explain exactly what we found.”

3. Media Confirm. “Did you have a chance to see the photos of the axle area on the report?”

4. Concern. “You came in for the oil change — that’s done. During the inspection we identified an additional finding I want to walk you through.”

5. Good News. “Good news is your suspension and steering components are otherwise in solid shape — ball joints, tie rods, and bushings all check out.”

6. More Good News. “More good news — we caught the axle boot leak before the joint itself failed. That’s a meaningful difference in cost and complexity.”

7. Risk → Solution. “If left as-is, the torn boot lets the lubricating grease escape and lets dirt and water in. Inside of a few thousand miles that will destroy the CV joint, which can fail under acceleration and leave you stranded. The proper repair is to replace the CV axle assembly, inspect the inner CV joint and surrounding components, and verify alignment.”

8. Investment. “The total updated investment, including the oil change you already authorized, is $[XXX].”

9. Coverage. “Everything is covered by our 3-year / 36,000-mile nationwide warranty — parts and labor.”

10. Tee-Up. “With the axle work in progress I’ll go ahead and have the tech finish the remaining inspection items and I’ll loop back if anything else surfaces. Still the best number?”

PRO TIPFor 2nd-base findings, name what’s already healthy first.

Step 5 (Good News) on a 2nd-base call should always name healthy adjacent components. This prevents the customer from assuming “if this is bad, everything is bad.” You’re drawing a clean perimeter around the actual finding.

sections.title: 3 · Cooling — Thermostat Stuck Open (Cold-Driving Customer)
sections.html:
3 · Cooling — Thermostat Stuck Open (Cold-Driving Customer)

Scenario: Customer reports heater not getting hot. DVI confirms stuck-open thermostat. (The water pump example in Module 6 is the canonical cooling reference; this is a second pattern for the same system.)

SCRIPTBase-1 ISO · Thermostat

1. Warm Opening. “Hi [Customer], this is [Advisor] from [Shop]. Is now a good time to walk through what we found?”

2. Co-Review. “I personally reviewed the cooling system with our tech so I could give you a complete picture.”

3. Media Confirm. “Did the report with the thermal-reading photos come through okay?”

4. Concern. “You brought it in because the heater isn’t getting hot — that’s the symptom. We’ve identified the cause.”

5. Good News. “Good news is the water pump, radiator, and hoses all checked out clean — no leaks, no contamination.”

6. More Good News. “More good news — the cause is a single component, not a system-wide problem. That keeps the repair targeted.”

7. Risk → Solution. “The thermostat is stuck in the open position, which means the engine never reaches proper operating temperature. Beyond no heat in the cabin, this causes excess fuel consumption, increased emissions, and accelerated engine wear over time. The proper repair is to replace the thermostat and gasket and complete a partial coolant service to refresh the system.”

8. Investment. “The total investment for today is $[XXX].”

9. Coverage. “Covered by our 3-year / 36,000-mile nationwide warranty on parts and labor.”

10. Tee-Up. “While we have the vehicle we’ll wrap up the bumper-to-bumper inspection and I’ll reach out with anything else. Still the best number?”

sections.title: 4 · Transmission — Fluid Service (3rd-Base Preventative)
sections.html:
4 · Transmission — Fluid Service (3rd-Base Preventative)

Scenario: 75k miles, fluid never serviced. Maintenance recommendation on 3rd base after primary work is authorized.

SCRIPT3rd-Base Soft Sell · Transmission Service

1. Warm Opening. “Hi [Customer], one quick maintenance item I want to mention before we wrap — is now a good time?”

2. Co-Review. “I reviewed your service history with the tech — this isn’t a problem today, this is a protect-your-investment item.”

3. Media Confirm. “You should see this in the maintenance section of the DVI — the transmission service status is highlighted yellow.”

4. Concern Framing. “Your vehicle has 75,000 miles and the transmission fluid hasn’t been serviced since new. Manufacturer interval is 60k.”

5. Good News. “Good news is the transmission is operating smoothly with no codes and no slippage — everything works.”

6. More Good News. “More good news — servicing the fluid now keeps your lifetime protection plan intact and prevents the most expensive repair on the vehicle.”

7. Risk → Solution (soft). “Old fluid loses its protective additives and starts depositing varnish on internal components. Left long enough, that leads to shifting issues and eventually transmission replacement — a multi-thousand-dollar repair. The service is a full fluid exchange with manufacturer-spec fluid and a filter replacement where serviceable.”

8. Investment. “Adding this to today’s work moves your total from $[XXX] to $[XXX].”

9. Coverage. “Service is covered by warranty and keeps your lifetime protection plan active on the transmission.”

10. Tee-Up. “If today doesn’t work for you we can absolutely schedule it for next visit — just want to make sure you know it’s on the horizon. What would you like to do?”

PSYCHOLOGY DECODERWhy 3rd base ends with a real opt-out.

Step 10 on a 3rd-base call explicitly offers to defer. This isn’t weakness — it’s positioning. Maintenance items are not safety items, and pressuring a maintenance sale erodes the trust you built on 1st base. Giving the customer the deferral option increases the same-day authorization rate because it removes the salesperson energy.

sections.title: 5 · Electrical — Battery + Alternator (Bundled Finding)
sections.html:
5 · Electrical — Battery + Alternator (Bundled Finding)

Scenario: Customer reports slow crank. DVI: battery testing at 60% capacity, alternator output borderline at idle.

SCRIPTBase-1 ISO · Charging System

1. Warm Opening. “Hi [Customer], this is [Advisor] from [Shop]. Is now a good time to go over the diagnostic results?”

2. Co-Review. “I just reviewed the charging-system test results with our tech to make sure I can walk you through them accurately.”

3. Media Confirm. “Did you receive the digital report with the battery load-test and alternator-output numbers?”

4. Concern. “You came in because of the slow cranking. We’ve tested the full charging system and identified the cause.”

5. Good News. “Good news is the starter motor itself is testing strong — that’s a major component that’s in good shape.”

6. More Good News. “More good news — we caught this before the vehicle stranded you. A no-start in a parking lot is a much worse day than this conversation.”

7. Risk → Solution. “The battery is testing at 60% of its rated capacity — it’s past serviceable life. And the alternator is producing output below the manufacturer threshold at idle, which is why the battery isn’t being properly recharged on short drives. Left as-is, this is a no-start event waiting for the right cold morning. The proper repair is to replace the battery with a manufacturer-spec unit and replace the alternator, then re-test the full charging system under load.”

8. Investment. “The total investment for today’s work is $[XXX], parts and labor.”

9. Coverage. “Battery comes with a manufacturer pro-rated warranty and the alternator and labor are covered under our 3-year / 36,000-mile nationwide warranty.”

10. Tee-Up. “We’ll wrap the rest of the bumper-to-bumper while we’re in there and loop back with anything else. Still the best number?”

sections.title: 6 · Tires — Replacement Set (Weather-Sensitive Framing)
sections.html:
6 · Tires — Replacement Set (Weather-Sensitive Framing)

Scenario: All four tires at 3/32. Customer in a wet-winter region.

SCRIPTBase-1 ISO · Tires

1. Warm Opening. “Hi [Customer], this is [Advisor] from [Shop]. Is now a good time to go over what we found?”

2. Co-Review. “I personally measured the tires with our tech and pulled the load-rating and date-code data so I could give you a complete picture.”

3. Media Confirm. “Were you able to see the tire-tread photos on the inspection report?”

4. Concern. “You came in for a check on the tires — we’ve fully inspected them.”

5. Good News. “Good news is the wear pattern is even, which means your alignment is in good shape and we don’t have a separate suspension issue contributing.”

6. More Good News. “More good news — we have manufacturer-quality tire options that fit your vehicle in a price range that protects both safety and budget.”

7. Risk → Solution. “All four tires are at 3/32-inch tread depth. The legal minimum is 2/32, but real-world stopping distance on wet pavement degrades significantly below 4/32. With the rainy season ahead, the risk is meaningfully longer stopping distance in exactly the conditions that need it most. The proper recommendation is a matched set of four new tires with a road-force balance, mount, and a 4-wheel alignment.”

8. Investment. “The total investment for the recommended tire package, installation, and alignment is $[XXX].”

9. Coverage. “Tires carry the manufacturer’s road-hazard and tread warranty, plus our 3-year / 36,000-mile labor coverage.”

10. Tee-Up. “Once you give the green light we’ll have the tires here within a few hours so we can finish today. Still the best number?”

PSYCHOLOGY DECODERWhy we tie tires to weather instead of legality.

“Legal minimum” is permission language — it tells the customer they can wait. “Wet-pavement stopping distance” is risk language. Always frame tires against the customer’s actual driving conditions, not the legal floor.

sections.title: 7 · Alignment — Pull / Premature Wear (Inspection-Triggered)
sections.html:
7 · Alignment — Pull / Premature Wear (Inspection-Triggered)

Scenario: Customer reports the vehicle pulls right. DVI confirms left-front toe out of spec, inside-edge tire wear visible.

SCRIPTBase-1 ISO · Alignment

1. Warm Opening. “Hi [Customer], this is [Advisor] from [Shop]. Is now a good time to walk through the inspection?”

2. Co-Review. “I reviewed the alignment readings and the tire-wear evidence with our tech so I could give you the full story.”

3. Media Confirm. “Were you able to see the alignment-spec sheet and the tire-wear photos on the report?”

4. Concern. “You brought the vehicle in for the pulling-right concern. We’ve identified the cause.”

5. Good News. “Good news is the steering and suspension components themselves are mechanically sound — this isn’t a worn-part problem.”

6. More Good News. “More good news — we caught this early enough that the tires still have usable life. Another few thousand miles and you’d be buying tires too.”

7. Risk → Solution. “The left-front toe is out of specification — that’s what’s causing the pull, and it’s what’s scrubbing the inside edge of the tire. Left as-is, you’ll need a full set of tires inside of 5,000 miles, plus the steering input it takes to keep the car straight increases driver fatigue and impacts emergency handling. The proper repair is a four-wheel alignment to manufacturer spec with a printed before-and-after report.”

8. Investment. “The total investment for today’s alignment service is $[XXX].”

9. Coverage. “Alignment work is covered by our service warranty and includes a free re-check inside 30 days if anything feels off.”

10. Tee-Up. “While the vehicle’s on the rack we’ll finish the remaining inspection items and let you know if anything else surfaces. Still the best number?”

sections.title: 8 · A/C — Low Refrigerant + Leak Test (Heat-Sensitive Framing)
sections.html:
8 · A/C — Low Refrigerant + Leak Test (Heat-Sensitive Framing)

Scenario: Customer says A/C blows warm. Diagnostic: low charge with UV-dye-confirmed leak at condenser.

SCRIPTBase-1 ISO · A/C System

1. Warm Opening. “Hi [Customer], this is [Advisor] from [Shop]. Is now a good time to go through the A/C diagnosis?”

2. Co-Review. “I reviewed the leak-test results with our tech — we ran UV dye through the system and pulled a vacuum to confirm.”

3. Media Confirm. “Did you receive the photos showing the leak source on the digital report?”

4. Concern. “You brought it in because the A/C is blowing warm — we’ve identified exactly why.”

5. Good News. “Good news is the compressor and the evaporator both test as functional — those are the most expensive components and they’re fine.”

6. More Good News. “More good news — we pinpointed the leak source on the first test, which means we know exactly what to repair instead of replacing components on guesswork.”

7. Risk → Solution. “The refrigerant charge is critically low because the condenser has a confirmed leak. If we just recharge it without fixing the leak, you’ll be back inside a few weeks with the same problem — and refrigerant venting to atmosphere is both environmentally and legally a concern. The proper repair is to replace the condenser, replace the receiver-drier, evacuate the system, and recharge to manufacturer specification with verified leak test.”

8. Investment. “The total investment is $[XXX], parts and labor included.”

9. Coverage. “Fully covered by our 3-year / 36,000-mile nationwide warranty on parts and labor.”

10. Tee-Up. “Once you authorize we’ll order the parts and finish the full inspection while we wait. Still the best number?”

PRO TIPRefuse the “just recharge it” ask.

Customers will often ask for “just a refill” when the diagnosis is a leak. Politely refuse. A recharge without leak repair is a 3-week fix — and the customer comes back angry. Reframe: “I’d be doing you a disservice by recharging a system that we’ve already confirmed has a leak. Let me fix it once, right.”

sections.title: The Cross-Library Anatomy — Why Every Example Hits the Same Beats
sections.html:
The Cross-Library Anatomy — Why Every Example Hits the Same Beats

BeatConstant Across All ExamplesWhat Changes Per Repair
1 Warm OpenPermission framing, name use.Time of day / context.
2 Co-Review“I personally reviewed with the tech.”Which system / component reviewed.
3 Media ConfirmLocks in DVI evidence.Which photos / readings to point to.
4 ConcernHonors the original reason for visit.The specific symptom language.
5 Good NewsNames healthy adjacent components.Which adjacent components to name.
6 More Good NewsReframes finding as a save / early catch.The specific “could have been worse” angle.
7 Risk → SolutionRisk explicit, solution proper, no shortcuts.The technical content of the repair.
8 InvestmentSingle number, parts & labor.The actual dollar amount.
9 CoverageWarranty named without pause.Any category-specific warranty (tires, battery).
10 Tee-UpConfirms contact, sets up next base.The specific tee-up language.
COACH’S CORNERDrill one example per morning standup.

Rotate through all 8 (plus water pump = 9) over a two-week cycle. Advisor delivers the chosen example from memory; coach scores against the 10-beat anatomy. By the end of Phase 2, every advisor can deliver every category cold. By Phase 3, the structure is so internalized that brand-new repair categories slot in automatically — advisors just plug the new technical content into the known structure.

MASTERY CHECKLibrary Mastery — the Pro Gate

An advisor is Library-certified when they can deliver any 3 randomly-selected examples from this library, back to back, from memory, with all 10 beats present and the Risk step explicit on every one. Coach signs off. This is one of the gates between Closer (Level 3) and Pro (Level 4).
